channel.title: Blog on Fengyu WANG
channel.description: Recent content in Blog on Fengyu WANG

item.title: A Bank Teller Existential Crisis
item.description: The first symptom of an existential crisis is a disturbance in the perception of time. When someone has sat at the same window for six years, time is no longer a linear river. It becomes an unmarked expanse of water. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: A Programmer Complete Problem-Solving Framework
item.description: The methodology programmers develop through debugging and writing code can be applied to nearly every domain. It is not about syntax or frameworks, it is about a structured approach to defining, decomposing, and solving problems. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: About Me
item.description: My work is built on a core belief: every complex problem can be broken down through a clear logical framework, analysed efficiently with the help of AI, and turned into systematic solutions that are reusable and actionable. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: AI Development Creates Economic Urgency
item.description: The rapid development of AI inevitably creates economic urgency. Its direct source is the mismatch between the speed of technological substitution and the speed of social adaptation. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Being Rational, Objective, and Honest
item.description: There is a structural contradiction between offering rational, honest consulting services and making easy money. Honesty itself is a sales barrier because the truth is often not what clients want to hear. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Businesses with Long-Term High Margins
item.description: Businesses that sustain high margins over the long term all revolve around a few deep and persistent drivers of human nature. The first driver is greed. In ancient times it was gambling houses, in modern times it is finance. Stocks, crypto, futures, derivatives. No matter how sophisticated the financial instrument, the underlying psychological mechanism is the same: people believe they are smarter than others, that they will profit where others lose.

item.title: Data Asset Awareness
item.description: Why are spreadsheets created by business development teams always messy? The answer is not carelessness or poor data literacy. It is a structural disconnect between the BD mindset and the standardisation required by data engineering. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Elon Musk Five-Step Working Method
item.description: The value of Elon Musk five-step method is not its novelty but how it distils key engineering principles into actionable steps with a clear priority order. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Excel Is Two-Dimensional
item.description: Excel uses rows and columns because two-dimensional tables are the easiest structure for the human eye and brain to understand. But the real world is not two-dimensional. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Execution Without Business Sense
item.description: Many people fail in business not because they lack execution but because they have execution without business sense. The stronger the execution, the faster they lose money when heading in the wrong direction. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Fair Profit Is Not Greed
item.description: There is a clear line between marking up and ripping someone off, but most people draw it in the wrong place. Charging ten times cost is not necessarily wrong, but charging nothing over cost means you are losing on yourself. Many people have a fundamental misunderstanding about profit. They equate markup with immorality. But this equation ignores a basic fact of business: reasonable profit is not exploitation. It is recognition of value and compensation for risk.

item.title: Fake It Till You Make It: Taking the First Step
item.description: The logic of taking the first step runs counter to most people instinct. The first step is never about being ready, it is about claiming your position first and learning to stand once you are there. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: First Principles of Making Money
item.description: The first principle of making money is creating value. Following this logic chain reveals a complete framework: creating value requires solving problems, solving problems requires identifying real needs through practice, iterating fast, validating models, and building systems that scale. The first principle of making money is creating value, not selling time. The first principle of creating value is solving problems, not providing cheap labour. The first principle of solving problems is identifying real needs, not self-indulgence.

item.title: Guinea-Bissau: A Case Study in State Fragility
item.description: Guinea-Bissau is an extreme example of governance failure. Since independence in 1974, the country has experienced multiple coups and periods of political turmoil, leaving state capacity extremely weak. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Information Gaps and Riding the Wave
item.description: Information asymmetry and leverage thinking are two of the most fundamental yet effective profit logics in business. They can be used separately or combined into a powerful strategy. The essence of information asymmetry is simple: you know something others do not, or you can get that knowledge at a lower cost than they can. In practice, this is not about keeping secrets. It is about efficiency. Leverage thinking means borrowing existing resources, channels, or results to lower your own cost of acquisition.

item.title: Open Source Tools That Replace Commercial Software
item.description: There is a class of excellent open source tools that can replace commercial software for web development, document processing, data management, and image hosting. They are all open source, self-hostable, and integrable with personal knowledge systems. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Premium Job Consulting on the Wrong Platform
item.description: Selling premium services on a low-price platform is essentially a channel mismatch. It is not that your service is bad. It is that you are selling the wrong thing in the wrong place. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Pricing Strategy Guide for Every Platform
item.description: Pricing is not cost-plus. It is a dynamic decision based on channels, customer psychology, and competitive landscape. Different platforms have completely different user behaviours that require different pricing strategies. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Probability and Business Promises
item.description: The willingness to offer an extreme guarantee to screen customers and then profit through probability and information asymmetry is a remarkably effective business strategy. Breaking this down reveals three layers. The first layer is the screening mechanism. Only people who genuinely need the service and believe in the technology will respond. Hesitant people, sceptics, casual enquirers are automatically filtered out. This is using a promise to screen customers, rather than using advertising to attract everyone.

item.title: Protecting Trade Secrets with AI-Generated Code
item.description: When building business tools with AI-generated code, the core principle is to completely separate programme functionality from access permissions. Employees get the interface, you keep the verification logic and keys. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Rent Extraction vs. Rent Creation in Regulation Theory
item.description: Two types of intermediaries play fundamentally different roles in economic activity. One type creates value, the other distributes value. The criterion for distinguishing between them defines how we understand the source of real value in markets. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Rock n Roll Is More Important Than Cannons
item.description: If we use two sounds to metaphor the logic of social operation, cannons represent hard commands and rules, while rock n roll represents the impulse for expression and creation. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Separating Business and Personal Accounts
item.description: Running a business and creating content are not in conflict. In fact, they complement each other. The two directions serve different markets and different customer bases, so there is no confusion. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Social Experiment Reality Shows
item.description: Locking real people in an enclosed space, throwing them a crisis scenario, and letting them solve problems using their genuine personalities, knowledge, and experience. This format has existed in television and experimental theatre for years. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Stay Honest in a World That Prefers You Did Not
item.description: Staying honest and clear-headed at work often earns you a strange label: being told you have low EQ, that you are too rigid. This feedback can make you doubt yourself, but the truth it reveals is exactly the opposite. The people calling you low EQ are exactly the ones whose path to muddy waters you are blocking. Break it down: when they say you have low EQ, what they really mean is you are too honest, too principled, too hard to fool, too hard to drag into their shady dealings.

item.title: Supply Is Strong, Demand Is Weak
item.description: The core contradiction in the current economy is structural: supply exceeds effective demand. This is not absolute overcapacity across all industries, but a differentiated, squeezing imbalance. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Surface-Level Experience vs. Core Capabilities
item.description: In the workplace, capability management can be strategically divided into two layers: surface-level experience and core capabilities. Use the surface layer to build trust, protect the core layer as your competitive moat. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: The Definitive Technical Research Workflow
item.description: The most common failure mode in technical research is not choosing the wrong technology. It is lacking structure in the research process itself. A closed-loop workflow dramatically reduces the chance of failure. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: The DM-Only Phenomenon on Xiaohongshu
item.description: Posts asking for recommendations on Xiaohongshu almost universally get replies saying DM me. This looks like information opacity on the surface, but underneath it is a complete business logic and platform rule system. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: The Palantir Ontological Approach
item.description: The uniqueness of the Palantir methodology is that it does not rely on complex mathematical functions to fit data. Instead, it builds a knowledge graph based on ontology, defining real-world objects as nodes and connecting their true relationships. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: The PDCA Cycle
item.description: There is a problem-solving method that has been repeatedly validated in practice: first study the known methods, then try them, identify problems, adjust the approach, and keep iterating until the problem is solved. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: The Rapid Development of Artificial Intelligence
item.description: The rapid advancement of AI has a highly differentiated impact on different economies. For economies that rely on large-scale labour, this impact first manifests as economic urgency. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: The Shi Yuzhu Business Logic
item.description: Shi Yuzhu business logic can be condensed into one sentence: do not educate, do not debate, do not change. Only identify, only satisfy, only close the deal. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Three Core Corporate Governance Dilemmas
item.description: Three common dilemmas in corporate governance all point to the same root cause: the mismatch between institutional design and human nature. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Time Moves Fast and Slow at the Same Time
item.description: Nearly every office worker experiences this split perception of time: the day flies by, yet every minute feels like it is dragging. There is a clear psychological mechanism behind this feeling. From a psychological perspective, these two feelings have completely different causes. Time feels fast when your brain is not truly processing anything. Meetings, replying to messages, filling forms, busywork, scrolling on your phone. None of these require deep thinking. Your brain is on autopilot.

item.title: Trust Cost Determines Price
item.description: The same person, the same service, the same product can sell for dramatically different prices in different channels. This is not an illusion. It is determined by several clear economic principles. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Using PDCA to Get Better AI Results
item.description: The seemingly simple act of asking an AI a question can be systematically managed. Incorporating AI prompting into the PDCA cycle turns random hit-or-miss queries into a standardised, repeatable, and controllable closed-loop process. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Why HTML Beats Word for Your CV
item.description: Using HTML and CSS for your CV solves every formatting problem Word creates. Every pixel can be precisely controlled, layouts are consistent across devices, it is responsive, version-controllable, and exports perfectly to PDF. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Why Low-End Customers Keep Finding You
item.description: If a product or service consistently attracts low-quality customers with limited budgets, the problem is usually not the customers. It is the way the product is presented. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Workplace Survival to Entrepreneurship
item.description: The transition from workplace survival to independent entrepreneurship is essentially an upgrade in cognition. What most people accumulate at work is the ability to do a job well, not the ability to run a business. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]

item.title: Zero-Sum Games, Arms Races, and Prisoner Dilemmas
item.description: What people commonly call involution has several precise theoretical models in academic discourse. They explain the same phenomenon from different angles: everyone works harder, yet no one improves their position. [Full article content in Chinese. Replace with English translation.]
